meta.title: Oral Surgery Practice Software | Surgical Coordination & Clearance Tracking | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to oral surgery practice operations. Surgical case coordination. Medical clearance tracking. IV sedation scheduling. Insurance authorization for extractions, implants, and jaw surgery.

hero.subhead: Surgical cases need pre-operative clearance tracked to completion. IV sedation scheduling requires anesthesia confirmation and medical clearance. Insurance authorization for hospital-based oral surgery requires extensive documentation. clinIQ manages oral surgery's coordination workflow from consultation to recovery.

sections.content:
Oral surgery practices perform procedures ranging from simple extractions under local anesthesia to complex orthognathic surgery under general anesthesia requiring hospital admissions. The operational requirements scale with procedure complexity — but even 'simple' oral surgery has coordination requirements that most practices manage manually.

The average oral surgery practice faces:

• IV sedation cases requiring medical clearance, fasting instructions, driver coordination, and anesthesia confirmation before scheduling is complete
• Hospital and ASC-based procedures requiring privileges, pre-admission testing, and facility coordination
• Medical-dental crossover cases — patients on anticoagulants, bisphosphonates, or immunosuppressants who require physician coordination before any surgical intervention
• Insurance authorization for implants, bone grafts, orthognathic surgery, and hospital-based extractions — each with different documentation requirements
• Post-operative monitoring for extended recovery cases — wisdom teeth, implants, jaw surgery — where complication recognition requires systematic follow-up

Most oral surgery practices manage this coordination through phone calls, sticky notes, and staff memory. Cases that should be straightforward are delayed by missing clearances. Authorization denials occur because documentation isn't organized. Post-operative calls go unmade because there's no systematic follow-up tracking.

Oral surgery case coordination begins at the consultation and continues through scheduling, pre-operative preparation, surgery, and recovery. Each step has requirements that must be completed before the next can proceed.

**Consultation to scheduling:**

Diagnosis and treatment plan documented. Procedure complexity assessed — office vs. ASC vs. hospital. Anesthesia type confirmed — local, nitrous, IV sedation, general. Insurance verification and benefit determination. Pre-authorization required? If yes, initiated at consultation or same day. Clearance requirements identified.

**Pre-operative checklist by procedure:**

Simple IV sedation extraction: Medical history reviewed, contraindications checked (anticoagulants, bisphosphonates, immunosuppression), fasting instructions provided, driver confirmed, blood pressure screened.

Complex impaction/multiple extractions: Panoramic X-ray, CBCT if nerve proximity concern. IV sedation clearance. Medical clearance if high-risk patient. Pre-op labs if indicated.

Implant placement: Implant planning (CBCT for bone assessment), implant size and system selection, bone graft if needed (with separate authorization), implant ordered for case.

Orthognathic surgery: Orthodontic collaboration, surgical planning with orthodontist, model surgery or virtual surgical planning, medical clearance, pre-admission testing, hospital scheduling.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Case pipeline: Every scheduled procedure with pre-operative checklist completion status. Authorization status: Required authorizations tracked from submission to approval — case not confirmed until auth received. Missing items: Cases with incomplete preparation flagged for coordinator action before they become day-of-surgery crises. Equipment and implant confirmation: Case-specific implants ordered and confirmed.

Oral surgery sits at the intersection of medicine and dentistry — the patient population includes elderly patients with multiple comorbidities, medically complex patients on high-risk medications, and patients who haven't seen a physician in years presenting with dental emergencies. Medical clearance decisions are clinically important and administratively demanding.

**High-risk medication scenarios requiring physician coordination:**

Anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran): Surgical decision requires weighing bleeding risk of continuing anticoagulation against thromboembolic risk of stopping. Simple extractions often proceed with anticoagulation continued; complex surgery may require interruption with physician guidance. INR must be checked within specified period if warfarin.

Bisphosphonates (alendronate, zoledronic acid) and denosumab: Medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) risk assessment. Duration of use, route of administration (oral vs. IV), and indication all factor into risk stratification. Complex extractions and implants may require drug holiday in consultation with prescribing physician.

Immunosuppressants (prednisone, methotrexate, biologics): Infection risk, wound healing impairment, steroid supplementation considerations. Rheumatologist or transplant physician coordination for complex cases.

Diabetes: HbA1c review for complex cases — uncontrolled diabetes impairs wound healing and infection resistance. Clearance from PCP or endocrinologist for complex surgery in patients with HbA1c >8%.

Cardiac devices: ICD and pacemaker — electrocautery restrictions. Device representative consultation or cardiologist clearance.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Clearance required: System flags cases requiring medical clearance based on documented medications and comorbidities. Clearance status: Clearance requested, received, or pending — visible without manual tracking. Clearance expiration: Clearance letters typically valid 30-90 days — expiring clearances flagged before surgical date.

IV sedation is the most common anesthesia approach in office-based oral surgery. Scheduling IV sedation cases requires coordination of pre-operative requirements, staffing (in-house anesthesiologist or CRNA vs. oral surgeon-administered sedation), recovery space, and post-discharge planning.

**IV sedation pre-operative requirements:**

NPO status: Nothing by mouth 6-8 hours for solids, 2-4 hours for clear liquids. Patient education must occur at consultation — not confirmed day-of. Confirmed adult driver: No Uber or taxi. Adult must remain at facility during procedure and accompany patient home. Patient cannot leave alone or use ride-share services.

Blood pressure screening: Hypertensive crisis contraindication to elective sedation. BP screened at check-in. Protocol for elevated readings must be defined.

Health history review: Completed and reviewed before sedation begins. Any new medications or health changes since consultation must be captured.

IV access: Staff trained in IV placement, or plan for difficult access. Anesthesia kit prepared and equipment functional.

**Staffing and scheduling requirements:**

In-house anesthesiologist or CRNA: Schedule must be coordinated. Anesthesia provider schedule confirmed before IV sedation cases are booked. Staff ratio: IV sedation cases require dedicated monitoring staff in addition to surgical assistant.

Recovery space: Recovery chair or room must be available for 30-45 minutes per patient. High-volume practices scheduling back-to-back IV sedation cases must account for recovery overlap.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Pre-op requirements checklist: Driver confirmed, NPO instructions acknowledged, blood pressure protocol set. Anesthesia schedule: Anesthesia provider availability confirmed before IV sedation booking. Recovery scheduling: Recovery time accounted for in scheduling to prevent overlap. Day-of checklist: Pre-op vitals, NPO confirmation, IV access, medication verification.

Oral surgery post-operative care includes both immediate recovery (first 24-72 hours) and extended follow-up (1-2 weeks for extractions, 3-6 months for implants). Complications — dry socket, infection, nerve injury, implant integration failure — require early identification for timely intervention.

**Post-operative monitoring by procedure type:**

Wisdom tooth extraction: 24-hour call protocol (hemorrhage, swelling, fever), 1-week follow-up for suture removal and socket assessment, 2-week follow-up for dry socket risk period completion.

Dental implant placement: 1-week post-op for healing assessment, 1-month for osseointegration tracking, 3-month for final osseointegration confirmation before crown restoration.

Bone grafting: 2-week wound check, 4-month bone density assessment before implant placement (for bone graft staging).

Orthognathic surgery: Hospital discharge follow-up 1 week, 2-week, 6-week, 3-month, 6-month — tracking occlusion stability, diet progression, orthodontic re-engagement.

**Complication monitoring:**

Dry socket (alveolar osteitis): Peak incidence days 3-5 post-extraction. Patients with severe pain onset at that interval should be seen. Outreach call at day 3 catches dry socket before patients suffer through the weekend.

Post-operative infection: Fever, progressive swelling beyond 24-48 hours, purulent discharge — early identification requires systematic follow-up protocol rather than waiting for patients to call.

Nerve injury: Persistent numbness or altered sensation requires tracking and potential specialist referral.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Post-op follow-up schedule: Automatic scheduling of follow-up appointments at the correct intervals. Outreach automation: 24-48 hour post-extraction wellness check. Complication documentation: When complications are reported or identified, tracked with response and resolution. Patient instructions: Post-operative instructions specific to procedure and anesthesia type sent to patient at discharge.

Oral surgery insurance authorization spans both medical and dental insurance — the complexity of which carrier pays for what procedure under what circumstances is a significant administrative burden for oral surgery practices.

**Common oral surgery authorization scenarios:**

Hospital-based extractions: When teeth are removed in an OR under general anesthesia (medically necessary or behaviorally complex patients), medical insurance typically covers the facility and anesthesia fees; dental insurance covers the extraction. Each carrier requires separate authorization.

Orthognathic surgery: Major jaw surgery (LeFort osteotomy, BSSO, genioplasty) is covered by medical insurance when medical necessity criteria are met — functional occlusal impairment, sleep apnea, facial asymmetry causing functional problems. Documentation requires cephalometric analysis, orthodontic records, clinical documentation of functional impairment, and often pre-operative orthodontic treatment completion.

Dental implants: Medical insurance covers implants only in specific scenarios — trauma, congenital absence, post-oncologic reconstruction. Standard dental implant coverage is through dental insurance, often subject to annual maximums. Authorization documentation for medical coverage requires specific medical necessity criteria.

Bone grafting: Authorization coverage depends on indication — ridge augmentation for implant preparation (dental coverage) vs. post-oncologic or post-traumatic reconstruction (medical coverage).

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Medical vs. dental coordination: Authorization tracking distinguishes medical and dental authorization requirements per procedure. LOMN generation: Medical necessity documentation for hospital extractions, orthognathic surgery, and implants in covered scenarios. Authorization status: Submitted, pending, approved, denied, appealed — visible without calling carriers. Benefit expiration: Dental annual maximum tracking — authorization expires at year-end, requiring re-authorization for January procedures.

**Related: [Oral Surgery Insurance: Medical vs. Dental Coverage for Surgical Cases](/blog/oral-surgery-insurance)**

**Week 1-2: Configuration**

Surgical case coordination workflow built for your procedure mix. Medical clearance protocol configured for your high-risk medication flagging. IV sedation pre-op checklist built to your office protocol. Insurance authorization tracking configured for your primary payers.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Front desk and scheduling staff training on case coordination pipeline. Clinical staff training on medical clearance workflow and complication monitoring. Provider training on authorization tracking and LOMN generation.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Case coordination pipeline visible immediately — no more missing clearances discovered day-of. Authorization tracking active. IV sedation pre-op checklist systematic.

Month 3: Post-operative follow-up compliance measurable. Authorization approval rate improving for hospital-based and orthognathic cases. Medical clearance gaps eliminated.

Month 6: Consistent authorization approval rates documented. Day-of-surgery cancellation rate due to missing preparation significantly reduced. Post-op complication identification rate improved.

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including case coordination, clearance tracking, sedation scheduling, post-op monitoring, authorization management
• Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How does the surgical case coordination checklist work?
faqs.a: Each scheduled case has a procedure-specific pre-operative checklist. For IV sedation cases: driver confirmed, NPO instructions sent, blood pressure protocol set, medical clearance obtained. For hospital cases: pre-admission testing ordered, medical clearance, facility privileges confirmed, anesthesia scheduled. Cases with incomplete checklists flag automatically and are not confirmed until complete.

faqs.q: How do you handle bisphosphonate and anticoagulant cases?
faqs.a: The system flags patients with high-risk medications based on health history documentation. Each flagged medication has a standard clearance pathway — anticoagulant cases generate a physician coordination request with procedure details and required guidance. Bisphosphonate cases trigger MRONJ risk assessment protocol. Clearance received is documented with date and provider. Clearance expiration is tracked.

faqs.q: How is IV sedation scheduling coordinated?
faqs.a: IV sedation cases are scheduled with anesthesia provider availability confirmed before booking. The pre-operative checklist (driver confirmed, NPO instructions acknowledged, blood pressure protocol, health history current) is tracked and visible to clinical staff. Day-of protocol includes vital signs, NPO confirmation, IV access, medication verification — each documented before sedation begins.

faqs.q: How do you handle medical vs. dental insurance coordination for hospital cases?
faqs.a: Hospital-based extractions under general anesthesia typically involve both medical insurance (facility, anesthesia) and dental insurance (extraction). clinIQ tracks authorization for each coverage separately, manages the distinct documentation requirements for each, and tracks approval status independently. Procedures are not confirmed until both authorizations are obtained if both are required.

faqs.q: What documentation is needed for orthognathic surgery authorization?
faqs.a: Medical insurance authorization for orthognathic surgery requires cephalometric analysis, orthodontic records, documentation of functional impairment (malocclusion affecting mastication, airway impairment, facial asymmetry with functional consequences), and typically completion of pre-surgical orthodontics. clinIQ generates the LOMN from a template that includes all required elements, with auto-populated clinical data.

Post-operative follow-up appointments are scheduled before the patient leaves surgery — not ad hoc. For wisdom teeth extraction: 1-week suture removal, 2-week completion. For implants: 1-week, 1-month, 3-month. The 24-48 hour wellness outreach is automated. Patients who report problems are tracked with response and resolution documented.

faqs.q: How does dry socket monitoring work?
faqs.a: Extraction patients are outreached at 24-48 hours for wellness check. High-risk patients (smokers, oral contraceptives, lower third molars, traumatic extraction) are flagged for day 3 outreach — the peak dry socket onset window. Patients reporting severe pain at days 3-5 are triaged for same-day or next-day assessment. The protocol ensures dry socket is caught before patients suffer through a weekend.

Two weeks. Week 1: workflow configuration for your procedure mix and authorization requirements, clearance protocol build, sedation checklist setup. Week 2: staff training by role and parallel testing. Week 3: live with support available for adjustments.

testimonial.quote: We used to discover missing clearances the morning of surgery. Now everything is tracked and confirmed before the case is ever confirmed. Our OR cancellation rate dropped to zero in two months.
